← the Atlas

R-4 — Local PTY daemon (@kolu/pty-host)

feature · budding ·accepted ·JUN 09, 2026

Decompose R-4 by hazard, not module — a low-risk in-process foundation (A1 structural refactor · A2 build/commit identity, both landed) whose only job is to shrink the one-way door, then B — the daemon itself, shipped whole with its safety and honest-state surface. Carries the

Contents

  • Fresh approach — phased by hazard, not module
    • The axes — most stable to most volatile
    • What this makes impossible by construction
  • The three units — A1 · A2 landed, B next
    • A1 — in-process structural foundation landed
    • A2 — build/commit identity + the staleness-key hash landed
    • B — the daemon, complete and safe next · the one-way door
  • The #1034 postmortem — production failure and hard constraints
    • Hard constraints for the redo
    • Execution lessons (process, not product)
  • Design notes that carry forward
  • History

The R-4 plan of record — local PTY daemonization + @kolu/pty-host. Split out of the parent remote-terminals plan on 2026-05-30 so the phases could be redesigned around the production restart failure recorded below; the parent keeps R-1/R-1.5/R-1.6 (shipped) and R-2/R-3 (post-R-4). Current state: A1 ( #1055 ) and A2 ( #1063 ) have landed; B — the daemon, the one-way door — is all that remains. Companions: the srv · pty ChromeBar rail (A2’s deliverable #3, shipped) and kolu-tui (a CLI client of the in-process pty-host — a separate plan that dovetails with B only later).

This note is the migrated + compressed docs/plans/remote-terminals.pty-daemon.html; the original’s constraints supersede any conflicting detail in its historical sections, and that ordering is preserved here.

Fresh approach — phased by hazard, not module

The plan is the output of a fresh /lowy (volatility-based decomposition) + /hickey (Simple Made Easy) pass over the whole feature, taking nothing about the discarded #1034 decomposition for granted. The thesis: decompose by concept and rank concepts most-stable → most-volatile — but let build order be set by a different fact: the process decouple is the only one-way door, the single step that changes runtime topology (IPC, a surviving process, single-instance, reattach). Everything is either before the door or part of it.

What’s allowed to be a separate phase is decided by hazard, not by module or convenience. The door creates the hazards — a dead daemon (the empty-canvas lie), a wire-incompatible one (a bricked server), a live restart that can lose the session (#1034), an outdated daemon the user can neither see nor act on. Everything that defuses or honestly surfaces those hazards is hazard-coupled to the door and ships with it; nothing in that surface may drift to a follow-up — that drift is the bug #1034 paid for. Only what is neither hazardous nor meaningless-without-the-door can live before it: the in-process foundation. The one move that matters most: identity/staleness is designed in as A2, before any daemon exists — a typed, build-enforced wire+behaviour closure, so a wire change can’t escape the staleness key by construction; currency is then a read-site derivation (staleKey !== currentBuildId()), never a stored boolean.

The axes — most stable to most volatile

Both lenses converge on one spine: eight concerns ranked by how often they change. The survivor holds only the slow end; everything per-deploy rides kolu-server’s lifecycle.

AxisRateBoundary that owns it
PTY ownership (the survivor core)rarely@kolu/pty-host + the daemon process: node-pty + the @xterm/headless mirror + the raw VT taps; single-instance pid-gate; cgroup-escape spawn. Zero providers in the survivor.
Wire contract + compat rule + identity closureper wire changeptyHostSurface (+ PTY_HOST_CONTRACT_VERSION + isPtyHostContractCompatible) co-located with a typed PtyHostIdentity, all inside @kolu/pty-host — so its source hash provably covers the wire+behaviour closure.
Identity values: staleness key vs navigable commitkey: per wire change · commit: per deploybuildId.ts: currentBuildId (staleKey) vs currentCommitHash (navigable ref), one typed pair — never conflated.
Connection livenessper deployDaemonHandle.state(): liveness-for-RPC vs liveness-for-display kept distinct; currency must not be frozen onto the connection value.
Provider DAG (fresh detection)per deploy — most-editedpackages/server providers.ts: a transport-agnostic ProviderRecord over plain channels seeded from the taps — never a PtyHandle.
Recovery sequenceper deployOne orchestration unit of step-values (captureSession → drainTerminals → respawn → finalize), shared by the forced + user paths — neither destructive path can be written kill-then-pray.
Kill vs natural-exit discriminationper deployOne order-safe killAndUnwatch(id) (abort the exit tap then kill); every kill caller uses it.
Health → UI projection (honest state)per UX tweakOne projection read by ChromeBar + DegradedCanvas + the restart dialog; the cell carries only the state enum, identity rides separately.

What this makes impossible by construction

The four production failures stop being review-time vigilance and become a failing unit test at the phase that owns the concept:

Production failureLens diagnosisKilled atHow
Mis-scoped staleness key (a wire change didn’t flip it)boundary leaked across packaging · concept inferred from a directory accidentA2The survivor’s closure is the package; the staleKey hashes its source, and a build-time guard fails if the contract reaches a wire/behaviour module outside it.
Over-prompting (whole-binary hash nudged every deploy)keyed on the wrong volatility axisA2The closure-scoped key flips iff a restart gains terminal-host code; a server-only change leaving it unchanged is a falsifiable test.
Data-loss restart (kill-then-pray)un-encapsulated sequence-volatility · a 7-concern god-functionBOne composed restart shared by the forced + user paths ships with the door — “kill before snapshot” is structurally impossible.
Empty-canvas lie (dead daemon → “you have no terminals”)failure presentation had no owning boundaryBHonest liveness as a value + a single owned health→UI projection ship with the door — never a follow-up.

Where the lenses disagreed, the resolutions: provenance is a one-shot buildInfo RPC into a signal, not a streaming cell (the health cell carries only {state}); recovery is one composed mechanism whose step-values parameterise what-to-preserve (the forced path’s captureSession returns an empty context); PtyHostIdentity is the single emitted value and currency derives from its staleKey at the read site; currentBuildId is memoised/process-stable in B, with a genuinely runtime-changing key deferred to R-3.

The three units — A1 · A2 landed, B next

No feature flags — each unit ships complete to master and leaves Kolu working. The boundary rule is asymmetric: hazard-coupled work must stay together (B is one PR despite its size), but hazard-free work may split however helps review (which is why A1/A2 is a clean cut).

A1 — in-process structural foundation landed #1055

The three structural moves, all in-process and identity-free: (1) extract the multi-client @kolu/pty-host primitive (node-pty + the @xterm/headless mirror + the raw VT taps: cwd · title · command-run · exit · foreground); (2) define the wire contract and serve it in-process, both inside @kolu/pty-host — ptyHostSurface + the major.minor compat rule, fulfilled over createPtyHost; (3) decouple the provider DAG over ProviderChannels / ProviderHooks / ProviderRecord — never a PtyHandle. After the #1059 link-family unification the serving is servePtyHost(deps) (transport-agnostic, returns the router) + directLink(router) (the no-wire member of websocketLink / stdioLink / directLink).

By serving the contract in-process, two of the door’s scariest surfaces were absorbed early: the contract boundary itself (the consumer speaks ptyHostSurface, not createPtyHost, so it survives the transport swap untouched) and the sync→async spawn race. A1 also reverted production to a pure in-process server, surgically removing all of #1034’s daemon machinery — there is no daemon in the codebase today. One observed cutover artifact: deploying A1 over a running #1034 daemon left it orphaned (it survives restarts by design, but A1 has no client for it) — reaped manually via systemctl --user stop kolu-pty-host.service.

A2 — build/commit identity + the staleness-key hash landed #1063

All the identity/hashing, isolated for its own review — low-risk to ship but the single most load-bearing correctness mechanism in the plan. Three deliverables, as shipped:

  • system.version extended with the typed PtyHostIdentity { staleKey, navigableCommit } (additive, optional — no contract bump).
  • Identity values + the closure-hash guard. currentBuildId (the staleKey — the nix hash of the package’s own source closure) and currentCommitHash (the navigable git ref) are nix-injected env (KOLU_PTY_HOST_BUILD_ID + KOLU_COMMIT_HASH); no dev fallback — kolu runs only under nix. A build-time test walks index.ts’s imports and fails if any reached wire/behaviour module is neither in-package nor on a small allowlist of stable deps — the #1034 mis-scope is a failing test from day one.
  • The consolidated srv · pty ChromeBar rail — see the rail prototype note. Built in its final two-column shape; in A2 the columns coincide (in-process) and that match is the acceptance signal; B only lets pty diverge.

Verify holds: mutating any pty-host source flips the staleKey; a server/client-only change doesn’t (no over-prompt). A post-merge prod break ( #1068 ) surfaced an unrelated migration rule worth keeping: any required new field on a persisted+streamed schema needs a migration/default (the fix was .default(0)).

B — the daemon, complete and safe next · the one-way door

The foundation is in master, so B is: the topology decouple + reattach-by-id + the composed recovery mechanism (incl. the #1034 fix) + activating the rail’s dormant honest-state branches — one hazard-coupled unit:

  • Topology — swap the in-memory link for a unix socket (serveOverStdio); the serving body is reused unchanged because A1 already put it in its final home. Relocate koluRoot/koluShellDir → kolu-shared (the daemon computes its own root). The kolu --stdio daemon in its own cgroup; the single-instance pid-gate (O_EXCL, held-until-death); the dev-flag exec-arg filter.
  • Reattach-by-id — server mints the UUID == daemon PTY id; on boot, list + join the saved session, no-spawn re-register; the decoupled DAG runs fresh against the surviving taps.
  • Currency — a read-site staleKey !== currentBuildId() derivation, never a stored boolean.
  • The recovery mechanism — one killAndUnwatch and one composed restart (captureSession → drainTerminals → respawn with the waitForPidGone lock-release barrier + load-aware timeouts → finalize), shared by the forced and user paths. Snapshot-before-kill + setSavedSession winning the autosave-cancel race, so a failed respawn leaves a restorable session, never a void. (Both axes of the #1034 loss condition are closed — the #1041 preference storm that contended with the autosave was fixed in #1050 .)
  • The daemon-state surface — the DaemonStatus cell (state enum only) + a one-shot buildInfo RPC; the update-pending nudge, the degraded canvas, the restart dialog, and the divergence of A2’s rail (pty flipping amber/red). No re-layout — B fills in branches A2 left dormant.

Why one unit: every bullet after Topology is hazard-coupled to it — shipping the door without the canvas, the forced-restart, the recovery mechanism, or the chip reintroduces exactly the postmortem’s failures. Cutover/rollback: deploying B onto an in-process prod is clean (the first boot just spawns a daemon); rolling B back orphans the surviving daemon — the same manual systemctl --user stop kolu-pty-host.service reap, mirrored.

Verify in three stages: (0) the cgroup-survival + socket-reattach spike re-proven on current master (run 2026-05-31 on a pu box, kernel 6.12/cgroup-v2: a systemd-run --user --unit= survivor and its child PTY outlived a systemctl --user restart of the parent; a fresh client reattached with in-memory state intact — passed); (1) test-first — daemon-reattach.feature and daemon-update.feature written red before topology lands; (2) the acceptance signal is the second deploy, not the first — survival/reattach/restart-race are first exercised only when B redeploys over a live B daemon with running agents.

Open follow-up — surface ↔ pty-host upstreaming

Now that the socket-serving glue exists, evaluate what should move into @kolu/surface itself vs stay package-local — the stdio-serve glue, the version/build-id handshake pattern, contract-compat helpers any surface-over-stdio consumer would want. A deliberate post-B evaluation in the original plan; its first instalment (the unix-socket transport + compat-helper extraction) is being executed on the kolu-tui Phase 1 PR #1084 .

The #1034 postmortem — production failure and hard constraints

The first R4c-UI build ( #1034 ) shipped the build-mismatch “update pending” nudge + a Restart local PTY daemon command. On production (Linux/systemd, 2026-05-30) the nudge fired correctly on a deploy; clicking restart then destroyed a live 20-terminal session and could not bring the daemon back. From the journal: killAll drained all 20 terminals first; the old daemon (13.5h old, 25G RAM on a thrashing box) took ~2min to exit; the respawn timed out at 30s; the user was left with zero terminals, a dead daemon handle, and an empty canvas indistinguishable from “you have no terminals.” No second daemon ever ran — the failure was the respawn losing the race against the slow old-daemon exit.

Hard constraints for the redo

  1. Restart must be recoverable, never kill-then-pray. Snapshot the session first; after respawn, auto-reattach/offer restore; if respawn fails, a loud recoverable degraded state with the session preserved — never an empty canvas.
  2. Wait for the old daemon to fully exit — the single-instance lock fights the restart. Wait on actual process exit (kill(pid,0) → ESRCH) before spawning, with generous load-aware ceilings.
  3. Timeouts must fit a loaded production box, not an idle dev one (20 heavy PTYs + a tsx cold-start under swap ≫ 30s).
  4. Never lie about state — an explicit connecting/degraded UI, never silent emptiness.
  5. A persistent PTY status indicator, always visible — connected · connecting · degraded/dead · update-pending (became A2’s rail).
  6. Key staleness on pty-host, not the whole binary — hash the @kolu/pty-host source closure so the nudge fires only when a restart actually gains something (became A2’s staleKey).
  7. Back up the session before a destructive restart (Export/Import session shipped via #1046 ).
  8. Fix the preferences storm ( #1041 , fixed in #1050 — coalesced writes).

The salvage of #1034 shipped constraints #1–#6 onto the PR and verified them live (recoverable ~600ms restart; honest DegradedCanvas; a deliberate server-only deploy did not nudge — the over-prompting bug fixed and confirmed). The fresh approach then discarded the lineage anyway: its defining mistake was letting the door’s safety surface drift into a follow-up, and the redesign makes that structurally impossible.

Execution lessons (process, not product)

From building + salvaging #1034 — pre-committed rules for the redo: typecheck every package before commit (a clean nix build is not proof CI passes — it never type-checks the server, #1049 ); never git stash in the worktree (use git show / a throwaway worktree); grep for unresolved conflict markers before committing a merge; confirm every Edit actually applied; don’t double-create GitHub issues on a flaky shell; never defer with a someday-issue — gaps go into the plan and get done in the PR; when the terminal contradicts itself, confirm via an independent path before “fixing” a phantom. Wire-shape rules that recurred: additive wire fields are optional + no contract bump (a required field force-restarts a surviving older daemon just to add a diagnostic); the identity surfaced to users must be the navigable one (commit SHA), with the source hash kept as the staleness key only.

Design notes that carry forward

The survivor is @kolu/pty-host only — node-pty fds + the @xterm/headless mirror + the raw VT taps + a unix socket. A kolu-server restart re-runs the providers against the surviving PTYs, so detection is never stale while the PTYs persist. Honest cost (accepted): metadata is no longer “warm” across a restart — a brief re-detection pass, trading warm-on-reconnect for freshness-on-deploy. Only a pty-host contract change (rare) forces terminal loss; a provider change (frequent) restarts the cheap layer with PTYs untouched. This corrects #1031, which daemonized a survivor that held the providers.

The cgroup mechanism (spike-verified). On Linux/systemd the daemon spawns via systemd-run --user --unit=kolu-pty-host (gated on INVOCATION_ID), landing in its own transient cgroup — a plain detached/setsid child does not survive on cgroup-v2 (KillMode=control-group walks cgroup membership, not the session — the #1031 Linux failure). macOS’s detached spawn already survives launchd. Caveats folded into B: linger must be on; absolute daemon path (minimal unit PATH); idempotent --unit reuse. Single-instance two ways: the unit name plus a pid-file (O_EXCL + liveness probe).

tmux/dtach considered and rejected: they only keep a PTY alive — no OSC-parsed taps, no headless snapshot for lazy-attach, no home for the provider DAG. You’d still build pty-host’s streaming layer next to tmux and inherit its session model on top.

History

  • R4a #1023 + R4b #1028 shipped clean as pure in-process refactors.
  • Two prototypes seeded the design: #994 (remote) proved “providers in the agent”, re-provisioned fresh per version; #1010 was a PTY-only local daemon — the thin-survivor model. The redo is their correct combination.
  • R4c first attempt #1031 dropped after a macOS prod failure: it daemonized pty-host plus the provider DAG, so the survivor served stale detection on every deploy — and staleness keyed on the inert pkgVersion constant, so outdated was always false and a 20-hour-old daemon was reused forever.
  • The volatility insight (Parnas/Lowy): “no legacy agent” ⊗ “tmux feel” conflict only when the long-lived component’s code changes — so the survivor must be the stable thin core, never the volatile providers.
  • R4c redo #1034 — the postmortem above; shipped + salvaged, then discarded by the fresh approach.
  • The fresh-approach redo (2026-05-30/31): #1055 (A1) landed the in-process foundation and removed every #1034 daemon artifact; #1059 unified the link family; #1063 (A2) landed identity + the closure-hash guard + the rail. Next: B — the only phase left.
